title: They Took Our New Car
summary: Juandalynn, the eldest, opens with her earliest memory of the movement: police seizing the family's new car when she was two or three, the only transportation they had, which the children later understood had been auctioned by the state of Alabama, along with a piece of the family property, to pay costs charged against the Montgomery bus boycott.

title: The House Was Bombed
summary: Donzaleigh, born just after their Montgomery home was bombed while her mother was pregnant with her, recounts how Juandalynn awoke singing in the blown-open house, the family's refuge with church member Margaret Brown, and her own infant memories of shaking, of church kisses, and of the Freedom Riders who came to stay.

title: The Freedom Riders in Our Living Room
summary: The sisters remember the Freedom Riders gathered in their Montgomery living room, the night Juandalynn cut Donzaleigh's braids, and the nearly a year the family spent in Margaret Brown's house before Donzaleigh's birth; Juandalynn recalls feeling alone as an only child until her sister arrived and they could talk in their own baby language.

title: A Disciplined Train Ride to Atlanta
summary: Donzaleigh recalls the threatening phone calls and the segregated train ride their father took the two girls on to visit the Kings in Atlanta, telling them they could come only if they asked for nothing; they rode the whole way from Montgomery without using a toilet or taking a drink, a lesson she carries that anything can be done with discipline, met at the old station by Uncle Martin.

title: A Memory of Fear
summary: Ralph III, born in 1959, says his earliest memory was fear: terrified the house would be bombed again as it had been in January 1957, and made more afraid when the move to Atlanta left him sleeping alone for the first time, the one child in his own room on the front of the house.

title: A Light in the Hall
summary: Ralph describes the hall light and open door his mother left for him, the wall he and his sisters knocked on to comfort each other, the back roads of Alabama and Georgia where they could not stop to eat or use a restroom, and the car that once followed them; over the fear lay an unspoken sense of God's protection, the Black angels his mother hung over the bed, and a nightly prayer to live until morning.

title: Did You Talk About the Fear
summary: Asked whether they spoke of the fear among themselves, the siblings recall their father telling them plainly he could be killed, the weekly tearful goodbyes, the dinnertime phone calls threatening the whole family, and their father's counsel that a sense of humor would carry them through life's hardest moments.

title: Children Adapt
summary: The three agree that, like children anywhere, they simply adapted, knowing the fear but not letting it dictate their lives because it was the only life they knew; Juandalynn, told she had to set the example for her siblings, was grounded by religion, by parents she could not have bettered, and by a Montgomery church community that walked her to school and embraced them.

part: The King Children, Our Constant Companions
title: Our Constant Companions
summary: Because other parents feared to let their children near the Abernathy home, the King children were the siblings' steady playmates, through school, ballet, and football; Donzaleigh, who turned introverted and played alone in a closet, recalls jumping up at the March on Washington when Dr. King spoke of his four little children, and the birthday TV surprise she could not keep secret.

title: Onto the Refrigerator
summary: Juandalynn remembers the tradition Uncle Martin began at their Montgomery home of lifting her onto the tall refrigerator to jump into his arms and then their father's, a treat that marked the men's return; Dr. King was at their house at least five days of the week, and in Atlanta the families swam together every Tuesday at the YMCA.

title: The Family Vacations
summary: The siblings recall Uncle Martin telling them how he had survived the Harlem stabbing, crawling over him as he showed where the letter opener had struck, and the vacations the two families always took together, to the 1964 New York World's Fair, to California, and in a leaner year to an Atlanta airport hotel, the boys, girls, and parents each in their own rooms.

title: He Must Have a Church
summary: Turning to the King-Abernathy partnership, the family describes how dependent Uncle Martin was on their father, the planner and strategist who pressed to go into Selma and Birmingham; lonely in Atlanta, King called nearly every day asking them to come, but Juanita held firm that her husband would move only with a church of his own, not as an SCLC staff member.

title: We Were Content in Montgomery
summary: The family recalls that the movement itself was formed the night their home was bombed, that their father pastored Montgomery's largest Black church where the boycott's mass meetings were held, and that Juanita refused to let him move merely to earn a living by speaking; only when the Kings helped find West Hunter Street Baptist Church did the family, content in Montgomery, agree to Atlanta.

title: Need, Love, and Friendship
summary: Dated to 1961, Ralph III reads the relationship as one of need, love, and friendship: his father drawn to Uncle Martin's oratorical gift, and Martin drawn to his father's courage as a World War II sergeant, his intellect, his two pastorates, his student demonstrations at Alabama State over housing and cafeteria conditions, and his sociology degree, all the skills King lacked and could lean on, in a partnership the histories reduce to the King and Abernathy show.

title: The Strategist and the Why
summary: The siblings credit their father, schooled in boycotts at Alabama State, with organizing the Montgomery ministers and the first mass meeting after Rosa Parks was arrested while E.D. Nixon was away, his colleague Jo Ann Robinson and their mother running off the flyers; in the partnership their father set the strategy, the what and when and how, and Uncle Martin gave the philosophy, the why, a pairing they liken to prophets sent out two by two.

title: The Civil Rights Twins
summary: Donzaleigh recalls the two men dressing alike from the same stores down to the same shoes and socks, calling themselves the Civil Rights Twins, a Capricorn and a Piscean who dreamed together, finished each other's sentences, and had only one disagreement ever; nothing their wives or anyone else could do came between them, closer than brothers.

title: All in Divine Order
summary: Juandalynn affirms that even A.D. King, Martin's brother, felt no jealousy of how close their father and Uncle Martin were, an arrangement Ralph III calls divine order; among the tokens of that bond was the solid gold Movado watch King gave their father, engraved to his dearest friend and closest associate.

title: Two Black Kids in the Park
summary: Ralph III recounts how he, Dexter King, and their sisters integrated Spring Street Elementary, the first integrated elementary in Georgia, where he was the undefeated running back and Dexter his blocker, the only two Black kids on the field; the integration followed the Selma to Montgomery march the children had walked, and the taunts soon followed, a classmate calling Juandalynn a cruel name.

title: We Were a Family
summary: Donzaleigh, small but spirited, describes pulling Juandalynn from class to rescue Ralph from a cruel teacher and standing up to the boy who threatened her sister, while Yoki King defended Juandalynn in turn; on the first day, angry parents and newspapers met them, and each Abernathy and King child was placed alone in an otherwise white class.

title: You Have to Be the Best
summary: Juandalynn recalls teachers, Ms. Gray and Mrs. Montague among them, who drilled into her that she had to be better than everyone because all eyes were on her, a weight made heavier after Dr. King was killed and she was told her father was now the leader; a principal even told a classmate's mother not to let her son host Ralph, with only the art teacher remembered as kind.

title: The Learning Tree
summary: Donzaleigh remembers her loving third-grade teacher Ms. Elliott, who pinned her A papers to the top of the class tree until the PTA objected and, in tears, taught her about racism by moving them to the bottom; after later teachers ignored her, their mother sent the children to a learning center where a white monk, Brother Clint, gave them the warmth the classroom withheld, and a Black librarian taught them French.

title: The Poster Children
summary: Ralph III explains the strategy behind involving children, that the young seek change while the old hope nothing changes, and that their father, asking others to bring their children to the movement, made sure his own took part; resilient and fearless, the Abernathy children became its poster children, present at every march, with the documented photographs other children lack.

title: Marching to Montgomery
summary: Donzaleigh recalls the 1965 drive down Highway 80 to walk the Selma to Montgomery march, the one-legged white marcher Jim Letherer on the front lines, the mass meeting crowded with movie stars including Sammy Davis Jr. and Peter, Paul and Mary, and watching from her father's lap as he counted the stars' hundred-dollar bills, the treasurer who always made sure the money reached where it belonged.

title: Thrown Into the Cars
summary: Donzaleigh remembers reaching the capitol for Dr. King's How Long, Not Long address, the tear gas and bottles at Cicero near Chicago in 1966 when the adults threw the children into the cars, and the James Meredith march where their mother defied a trooper to take Ralph to a restroom; their father, she says, wanted them above all to witness and be a part of history.

title: Little Ralph Is Going to Preach
summary: Ralph III describes resisting the pulpit because of what the movement had cost his father and Uncle Martin, serving instead in the Georgia House and Senate before accepting his calling and earning a doctorate in theology; his sisters recall him preaching full sermons to them at home on Sunday mornings from the age of four or five, his amen corner of two.

title: Born Singing
summary: Donzaleigh recalls deciding at the March on Washington to become an actress like the socially minded movie stars, safer than the dangerous work of their fathers, while Juandalynn says singing was simply her destiny, encouraged by Mahalia Jackson, who would stay at their home and marvel at the three voices the child could already produce; the marches gave them the freedom songs and spirituals to sing.

title: It Needs to Be Continued
summary: The interviewers begin to close, and the siblings protest that so much is left untold, their father's arrest, the Poor People's Campaign, the food stamp program he helped bring about, affirmative action, and the King holiday they say originated in his soul, prompting a plan to record a second part.

part: The Mule Train and the Fair
title: The Mule Train
summary: After the connection to Juandalynn in Germany is lost, the conversation resumes with Ralph III recounting his June 1968 arrest on the Poor People's Campaign Mule Train from Marks, Mississippi, stopped in Douglasville, Georgia, by Governor Lester Maddox along with all sixty-seven marchers and held overnight in a National Guard armory; nine years old, he told his worried parents that his father and Uncle Martin had done this all the time, while Donzaleigh, barred as a girl, longed to be arrested too.

title: FunTown and the Fair
summary: Donzaleigh recalls the privileges the boys were given, like the small plane ride the girls were denied, the Southeastern Fair where their farmer father and Uncle Martin let them ride and eat their fill, the FunTown amusement park closed to them that King wrote of in his Letter From Birmingham Jail, and the Sunday dinners and leftover communion grape juice the families shared each week.

title: The Bomb Went Off
summary: Donzaleigh remembers her father asking the congregation to bow in prayer the Sunday the Sixteenth Street Baptist Church was bombed, and how, running along the church afterward, she froze in terror until her brother came back and took her hand, leading her gently through her fear past the alley and the barbecue; she never used the church's public restroom again until Juandalynn's wedding, because that was where the girls had died.

title: Turn On the Television
summary: The siblings recount learning Dr. King had been shot when a friend's mother called and told their mother to turn on the news, Juandalynn screaming and calling Yoki as the families gathered and Andy Young told their mother to pack for Memphis; Donzaleigh, insisting King would survive as he had the stabbing, rode to the airport and began to cry only when she saw her mother and Coretta embrace on the tarmac, where Mayor Ivan Allen had brought the news of his death.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: Nothing Could Fill the Void
summary: Donzaleigh recalls her mother going home to sleep on Uncle Martin's side of the bed so Coretta could tell her children face-to-face, the body returning in a box, and their father sitting in the dark, depressed beyond belief, comforted not even by the pool table they had built for King; Ralph III says his own first fear at the news was for his father, who had shared the Lorraine Motel room with King.

title: It's Ralph, Everything's Going to Be Alright
summary: Ralph III relays his father's account of King's last moments: dressed first by their arrangement, he had stepped back inside for his cologne when the shots came, and reaching the balcony he cradled King, told Andy Young to fetch towels rather than cry out, and rode in the ambulance to hold King as he took his last breaths; by King's own design, Ralph David Abernathy became SCLC president by acclamation.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: You Cannot Kill the Dream
summary: Ralph III answers those who deem the movement spent after the assassination, arguing that bringing the poor to Washington to demand that the powerful feed the hungry and clothe the naked was itself a success, and that his father's words calmed a nation aflame, that nonviolence is the weapon of the strong, and that they might kill the dreamer but never the dream.

title: A Date With Destiny
summary: The siblings recall their father teaching that courage is going forward in the face of fear, the bomb threat that held the Memphis plane on the ground, and the Mason Temple night in 1968 when, taunted with death, King rose above it; warned by Medgar Evers's killing of what might come, the family understood the men lived on borrowed time, King leaving sweaters behind to be remembered by, having written of a date with destiny.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: More Bees With Honey
topic: How They Worked as a Team
summary: Donzaleigh frames her father's gift as keeping the background, calming SNCC and the other organizations into one united front so the movement could have a single head, the first time, she says, that Black Americans had truly organized where DuBois and Booker T. Washington could not; their father out-thought opponents by over-asking in demands, funded Andrew Young's run for Congress from the SCLC budget, and gave Daddy King the money for King's burial land.

title: Servant of All
summary: The siblings close on their father's giving: handed six million dollars in HUD money for Abernathy Towers, he deeded it to his church and kept nothing, telling their mother on his deathbed that his life had been about helping people, not making money; Donzaleigh recalls his last proud phone call before he collapsed, the children taking turns at his bedside to the end, and Ralph III seals it with the scripture that the greatest among you shall be servant of all.
